frameworks.name: self-consistent Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: perceptual inference causally conditioned on the preceding categorical choice

frameworks.name: late memory-noise Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: alternative attributing post-decision bias to memory noise alone

frameworks.name: cognitive dissonance
frameworks.one_line: analogy to subjective preference shifts after a commitment

core_question: How does making a categorical judgment systematically bias subsequent perceptual estimates of the same stimulus?
key_assumptions: perceptual inference can be causally conditioned on a prior choice
the observer enforces consistency between categorical judgment and estimate
biases reflect inference rather than purely late memory noise
manipulations: task sequence: categorical orientation judgment followed by orientation estimate
stimulus orientation relative to category boundary
key_findings: post-decision estimates are systematically biased away from the category boundary
high consistency between categorical judgment and subsequent estimate
bias pattern is hard to reconcile with late memory-noise accounts
self-consistent Bayesian model quantitatively captures the observed biases
interpretations: perceptual inference is causally conditioned on preceding choices
a universal self-consistency constraint may govern brain inference, paralleling cognitive dissonance
